He read the Council authorization at his terminal that evening. The session had ended at midafternoon and the formal document had arrived in his filing queue by the end of day. He read it twice. He filed the acknowledgment. The acknowledgment was a one-line form: *mandate expansion noted. Investigation proceeds under expanded authority. Liaison position: Corin Draven.*

He sent a copy to Lian. He sent a copy to Iris.

He sent a copy to Fay. Fay had been in the eastern communities for the past ten days — the coordination agreement's quarterly review, which she conducted in-person, staying at the eastern junction settlement for the duration. He sent it with a header: *mandate expansion authorized. Wanted you to see it before you're back.*

He sent a copy to Maret, marked: *The investigation will go through the eastern communities' territory. I wanted you to know before it began.*

He sent a copy to Vessa, with no header. The Congregation's position had been part of the Council session's record — he had summarized it in response to a Council question about outside support for the mandate expansion. Vessa had already said what she wanted to say. The copy was information.

He thought about sending a copy to Elara. She would already have the Council session's record — she tracked everything the Council authorized. She would have the document before he sent it. He sent it anyway, with a header: *authorized. Instrument design begins.*

He logged the filings. Five copies sent. Acknowledgment on record. Investigation authority confirmed.

He looked at the anomaly map. The coordinate was unchanged. The eastern field was unchanged. The entity was doing something thirty-five meters below the surface and further down, into the Layer, and now he had the formal authority to find out what.

He pulled up the Kassel Collection's instrument specifications on the secondary terminal. The prototype would take three to four months. Lian had said three as the optimistic estimate, four as the working assumption. He had four months before there would be an instrument ready to follow the signal below forty meters.

Four months was not immediate. Four months was a timeline with work inside it.

He opened the instrument specification file and went to work.

chapters.title: Chapter 6: Act II Closes
chapters.text:
Six weeks after the mandate expansion, the instrument design was sixteen pages of technical specification with eleven unresolved questions in the margins.

Lian's team had established the prototype's basic architecture in the first two weeks. The Kassel Collection's founding-period specifications provided the operating principle — the deep-substrate measurement technique the Archive's earliest monitoring teams had developed before the Reckoner network's installation had made deep monitoring unnecessary. The principle was sound. The materials were the problem. The founding period's instruments had used a substrate-coupling material that was no longer manufactured, had not been manufactured in sixty years, and whose chemical composition was documented in the collection's records but whose fabrication process had been lost with the team that had produced it.

Lian had assigned the materials problem to two members of her team who had substrate chemistry backgrounds. They had spent four weeks on it. On the twenty-eighth day, one of them had come to the weekly briefing with a partial solution — not the original material, but a substitute with equivalent coupling properties that could be produced with current fabrication methods. The substitute had different thermal characteristics, which meant the instrument's calibration protocol would need to be redesigned from the founding period's version.

"Three to four months," Lian said, at the sixth-week briefing. She said it the way she said things that were estimates: with the quality of a person who had run the calculation and had confidence in the range but not in a specific date. "The materials substitute is workable. The calibration redesign adds two to three weeks to the schedule. We're on track."

She did not add that no one had built an instrument like this in eighty years. She had noted it once, in the first week, and had not noted it again. The fact was in the record. It did not need to be a recurring statement.

Corin logged the update and sent the briefing notes to Iris.

---

The reconciliation framework's final formal session for the current period was on the seventh Thursday after the mandate expansion. It met in the small council annex rather than the main committee room — the same space the early reconciliation sessions had used, before the framework had formally constituted and moved to the committee room for its plenary sessions. Coming back to the annex was Vessa's suggestion. She had said, at the previous month's session: "The committee room is where the framework operates institutionally. The annex is where it started. I want the close-out session in the annex." No one had objected.

The room held eight people: Corin, representing the liaison position; Elara Venn, representing institutional oversight; Vessa and Joren, representing the Congregation; the two representatives of Dreya Cole's Teller territory, who had been part of the framework since its third session; Iris, as documentation registrar; and Maret, who had come from the eastern communities for the session.

Vessa presented the Congregation's position review. She stood when she gave the summary — she always stood, had done so from the first session as a kind of marker that this was formal speech, not conversation. Her voice was even.

"Twenty-two former fighters entered the reconciliation framework. Twenty-two have documentation on file. Of the twenty-two: seventeen have completed their individual reconciliation documentation, which includes personal statement, engagement record, and the liaison position's acknowledgment filing. Four are in active process — documentation is partial and sessions are ongoing. One — Gull — has not completed the personal statement stage but has attended eight sessions and has, in each session, contributed to the collective record."

She paused.

"The reconciliation is not complete. Gull's process has its own timeline. The four in active process have their own timelines. The framework's documentation acknowledges this — the close-out session closes the current period, not the process itself. The process continues." She looked at the table. "The framework has documentation on every person who entered it. That documentation is in the registry. It will be there when the process continues."

Iris noted the documentation status in the session record as Vessa spoke. Her hand moved steadily.

The Teller territory representatives presented their summary. The coordination with the eastern communities had produced two additional cross-references in the framework's record — the settlement documentation that Maret's office had provided, and the coordination agreement's operational record, which established the framework's formal relationship with the eastern community territory through which the investigation would pass. The Teller territory's participation in the framework had begun as a geographic concern — the reconciliation events had occurred partly in the Teller corridor — and had widened, over twenty months, into something closer to a peer engagement. The representatives had brought a written summary. Iris incorporated it into the session record.

Then Maret raised the eastern communities' request.

She did it at the session's closing-item point, where agenda items that had not been submitted in advance could be brought forward if the full membership consented to hear them. She had not submitted it in advance. She raised it at the table, standing, and said: "The eastern communities request formal representation in the reconciliation framework. Not Archive representation on our behalf — our own seat. As a party to the reconciliation."

Corin looked at her. Maret was strategic and had a long institutional memory — she had worked in coordination roles across three different eastern jurisdiction organizations before the coordination agreement with the Citadel had formalized her position as the communities' direct representative. She did not make procedural moves without knowing what they were for.

"The eastern communities are the territory where the harm occurred," she said. "Two hundred and sixteen people from those communities lost Resonance architecture at the Eastern Field Calibration coordinate. The harm's registry entries cite the eastern communities' records. The After column's documentation draws on eastern community sources. The investigation that is now underway will operate in eastern community territory." She looked at the table. "The eastern communities are a party to this reconciliation. We have been treated as context. We should have a seat."

The framework's existing members had the authority to expand their number by vote. This had been in the framework's founding documents — a provision Elara had insisted on, during the second session, against the argument that the framework's composition should be fixed to ensure consistency. Elara had said: *the reconciliation involves more parties than the ones in this room. The framework must be able to grow.*

The vote was unanimous. Maret was the eastern communities' representative in the reconciliation framework, effective the present session.

She sat down. She did not look like she had won something. She looked like she had done what needed to be done, and it had been done, and now the work continued.

---

The day after the session, Iris showed Corin the registry count.

She brought up the statistics view on the primary terminal — a view she had built into the registry's internal structure herself, a decade ago, before the registry had ever been used for anything like its current purpose. The statistics view showed the total number of entries by category, the cross-reference density, the number of external citations.

"The harm entries," she said. "Two hundred and sixteen. Each one has at least the basic documentation — name, date of harm, Resonance architecture status. One hundred and forty-seven have additional context entries — family record cross-references, After column content from the eastern communities' sources, liaison acknowledgment notations."

She scrolled.

"The After column: two hundred and three of the two hundred and sixteen have at least one After column entry. Some are brief — a notation, a cross-reference, a confirmed location. Some are extensive. Orri's entry has twelve lines. Bern's entry has seven. The thirteen without After column content — we haven't found sourcing yet. We haven't stopped looking."

She scrolled again.

"The Timelost entries: forty-one. The erasure entries: fourteen, from the documentation gaps the investigation identified. The investigation entries: thirty-seven logged findings, including the depth-profile finding and the Kassel Collection documentation. The reconciliation entries: the individual documentation for all twenty-two, the session records, the position reviews."

She paused.

"External citations: sixty-three. Sixty-three places where the registry cites a source outside the Archive's own documentation — the eastern communities' settlement record, the Congregation's founding document, the coordination agreement's operational record, the individual statements that came into the framework. Before fourteen months ago: zero. The registry cited only internal Archive sources."

She looked at the count for a moment.

"I have been building this for twenty-three years. In the last fourteen months, more people have added to it than in the previous twenty-two years combined."

He looked at the numbers. He thought about the registry the way it had existed before the investigation — the way Iris had described it in the first month, when he had first understood what she was building. A closed structure. The institution documenting the institution. Citations pointing inward.

"Does that change what you do?" he asked.

She was quiet for a moment. Not thinking about the answer — she already had the answer. Thinking about how to give it.

"It changes what the record is," she said. "What I do is the same — I document what needs to be documented, and I make sure what's documented can be found." She paused. "The record doesn't belong to the institution anymore. It belongs to everyone who has a citation in it. Bern has a citation. Maret's office has a citation. The Congregation's founding document has a citation. The founding period's monitoring team has a citation now, with the deep-monitoring note that Cross located." She looked at the statistics view. "I can't control what the record is once it has that many sources. I can only make sure the citations are accurate and the cross-references work and the thing is navigable by someone who needs to find something in it." She went back to the terminal. "That's the work. It's the same work. The record is different."

He stood at the edge of her office for a moment, looking at the terminal. The registry's statistics view had the quality of something you could look at for a long time and keep finding things in. Not because it was beautiful — it was a data summary, tables and counts — but because each number was a direction toward something that had happened, toward someone who had been part of what had happened, toward the years of work that had produced the entry.

He went back to the liaison office.

---

That evening, late, he read the pre-Reckoner instrument documentation for the third time.

Not the technical specifications — he had those nearly memorized now, the eleven unresolved questions in the margins as familiar as his own handwriting. He read the founding period's operational notes. The daily records of the first monitoring team, the team that had built the deep instruments and taken them to the substrate and measured what was below forty meters for the first time.

The notes were written in the Archive's founding-period formal register, which was drier than the current technical language — less hedged, more declarative, as if the team had not yet learned to be uncertain in print. The early entries described the instrument development. The middle entries described the field deployment. The late entries described the finding.

*Below 40 meters, the substrate signal changes character.* He had read this line enough times that it had become part of his working vocabulary — the way a line from a document you've lived with becomes part of how you think. *The deeper instruments registered something that did not match the surface-level model. We did not have the theoretical framework to characterize it. The second monitoring expedition was scheduled to investigate.*

And then the dissolution note. *Resources reallocated to the Reckoner network installation Phase I.*

He looked up from the documentation.

The first monitoring team had found it. Had found the signal below forty meters, had found that it didn't match the surface model, had written it down, had planned to investigate further, and had been dissolved before the investigation could happen. The institutional resources had gone elsewhere. The Reckoner network had been built. The deep-monitoring instruments had been decommissioned and the specifications had gone to the Kassel Collection and eighty years had passed.

In those eighty years: the extraction had begun. The Archive had become what it had become. The harm at the Eastern Field Calibration coordinate had occurred. Two hundred and sixteen people had lost Resonance architecture permanently. The reconciliation framework had spent thirty-one years not existing. The investigation had spent thirty-one years not being resourced. His father had reached the coordinate and had written *the difference is present, the difference is real* and had filed a note saying the resources didn't exist and had stopped.

And now he was sitting at the liaison office terminal at the end of the second Act, reading a note written eighty years ago by a monitoring team that had been dissolved, and the prototype instrument was sixteen pages of specification with eleven unresolved questions, and in three to four months it would be field-ready, and the signal was still at the coordinate, still going below forty meters, still doing whatever the entity had been doing there for fourteen months, for thirty-one years, for longer than the archive's memory reached.

He filed the documentation reference in the investigation archive. He added the founding monitoring team's note to the record — not as a new harm entry, not as a procedural finding, but as a historical document, something the investigation archive needed in order to tell the complete story of how long the Layer had been waiting to be looked at directly.

He closed the Kassel Collection file. He closed the investigation archive. He closed the anomaly map.

He stood at the office window.

The inner perimeter was quiet at this hour. The operational bay's amber lighting caught the shuttle's hull — the same shuttle, unmoving, as it had been since the deployment night fourteen months ago. The coordination teams used different vehicles now, the coordination agreement's logistics having produced a dedicated transport pool. The shuttle sat because no one had decided what to do with it yet, and leaving it in the bay was not a decision so much as an absence of decision, the thing that happens to objects when the context that made them necessary has passed.

He looked at it for a while.

The harm was documented and acknowledged. The Council motion was on record. The After column was at two hundred and three of two hundred and sixteen. The institutional structure had been partly redesigned — the protocol redesign was in its final month, the cross-classification review was ongoing. The 40-year gap was in the record. The Substrate Assessment Unit's Exchange relationship was in the record. Parren was gone. Derel had cooperated and walked away.

The reconciliation framework had a new seat. The Congregation's position had been revised. Twenty-two former fighters were documented. Gull was still working with the record.

The restoration rate was 15.3%.

The liaison position had formal substrate investigation authority. The instrument was being built. The founding period's deep-monitoring note was now in the investigation archive. The Layer was below forty meters and the entity was doing something in it that the monitoring could not follow and in three to four months there would be an instrument that could follow it.

He thought about the founding monitoring team's note. *We did not have the theoretical framework to characterize it.* They hadn't had one. Neither did he. The instrument would give him data and the data might give Lian a framework and the framework might give the investigation something to stand on. Or it might not. It might give them something that didn't match the model, the way it had not matched the first team's model eighty years ago, and the investigation would need to develop a new model.

That was the work. That was what the next months were.

He turned from the window. He put on his jacket. He looked at the office one more time — the terminal dark, the physical binder in its place on the shelf, the anomaly map printed and pinned to the board in the corner, the coordinate a small star in the eastern field's gradient.

The investigation was open.

The Layer was below.

The instrument would take four months to build.

He went home. He went back to work in the morning.
